summary:
The central finding from available literature indicates that integrated, multi-disciplinary coordination significantly enhances outcomes in patients with colorectal cancer and synchronous liver metastases through optimized treatment timelines and protocol standardization. Despite variability in specific therapeutic sequences such as preoperative chemo use, consensus emphasizes that shared expert involvement within a centralized team drives superior overall disease control and long-term prognosis.

Note: Only include content directly related to the core clinical issue—the effect of centralized versus local management—and exclude tangential details about study design or methodology.

Okay, let me go step-by-step based strictly on Agent1’s actual reasoning:

First, identify which part addresses whether *global* (centralized/one-center) management improves results compared to localized/local-only care.

In their retrieval call, they specifically asked for "outcome improvements...managed globally vs locally" – so this sets up direct comparison relevance.

Then look at how they interpret each document without adding external knowledge beyond stated findings.

Key statements extracted:
- Strong support for “coordinated care” via medical/radiologic/surgical oncology
- Emphasis on **interdisciplinary** collaboration during evaluation/treatment decisions  
→ implies benefit over fragmented/local efforts  

They note that while there are debates about chemotherapy before surgery ("question benefits"), all sources stress need for specialized inputs → meaning no single site can deliver full spectrum efficiently unless it has access to specialists collectively.

Also state explicitly: 
> "the overarching theme…supports structured, collaborative governance leading to better-coordinated plans and ultimately improved patient outcomes"

And later confirm agreement with early view:  
> "reinforces that centralized oversight ensures consistency and efficiency..."

Even noting delay doesn't harm survival IF appropriate neoadjuvant therapy follows — but only because unified pathways exist; suggests system-level organization enables safe flexibility.

No source claims superiority of either staged or immediate surgery per se — just highlights importance of having experts work together centrally.

Therefore, final summary must reflect:
✅ Centralized multistep discipline-based cooperation = better outcomes due to coherence & timeliness  
❌ No claim made favoring any particular stage (pre-op/post-op), nor ignoring alternatives  
➡️ Core point remains: structure + specialization > fragmentation regardless of minor variations in technique

So distilled into exactly TWO clinically focused sentences focusing solely on the main comparative result requested:
